standard_description: Correctly use frequently confused words (e.g., to, too, two; there, their).
subject: Language

khan_exercises.title: Frequently confused words: affect/effect
khan_exercises.description: Do you know when to use "affect" and when to use "effect"?

items.question_content:
**Identify whether to use _affect_ or _effect_ in this sentence.**

> Movies about outer space have a profound \_\_\_\_\_\_ on me.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
People often confuse the words "affect" and "effect":

* **affect** is a verb that means "to impact" or "to influence"

  *The loud noise **affected** the golfer's backswing.*

* **effect** is a noun that means "result" or "consequence"

 *One **effect** of the Industrial Revolution was the increased use of steam power.*

**Note:** Both "affect" and "effect" have other definitions, but these are the ones that people usually mix up.
The sentence needs a noun that means something like "result", so we should use **effect.**

khan_exercises.title: Frequently confused words: here/hear
khan_exercises.description: Can you identify the difference between the words "here" and "hear"?

items.question_content:
**Identify whether the word _here_ or _hear_ should be used in this sentence.**

> The ladder is right over \_\_\_\_\_\_.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
People often confuse the words "hear" and "here":

* **hear** is a verb that describes the perception of sound

 *I can **hear** the church bells ringing.*

* **here** is an adverb that describes an exact location

 *I found your wallet **here** in the couch cushions.*
The sentence needs a word that describes a *location*, so we should use **here**.

items.question_content:
**Identify whether the word _here_ or _hear_ should be used in this sentence.**

> Speak up! I can’t \_\_\_\_\_\_ you.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
People often confuse the words "hear" and "here":

* **hear** is a verb that describes the perception of sound

 *I can **hear** the church bells ringing.*

* **here** is an adverb that describes an exact location

 *I found your wallet **here** in the couch cushions.*
The sentence needs a verb that refers to *sound*, so we should use **hear**.

khan_exercises.title: Frequently confused words: there/their/they're
khan_exercises.description: Do you know when to use "there", "their", and "they're"?

items.question_content:
**Identify whether to use _there_, _their_, or _they’re_ in this sentence.**

> DeRay and Evgenii are hosting a game night at \_\_\_\_\_\_ house.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
People often confuse the words "their", "there", and "they're":

* **their** is a possessive pronoun that means something *belongs to them*

 *The hockey players brought **their** pads and skates to the rink.*

* **there** is an adverb that describes a *specific location* or the *existence* of something

 *The dog park is over **there** by the lake.*

* **they're** is a contraction of the words *"they are"*

 *Asparagus stalks are usually green, but sometimes **they're** white.*
The sentence needs to show *possession*, so we should use **their**.

items.question_content:
**Identify whether to use _there_, _their_, or _they’re_ in this sentence.**

> Look at that spaceship over \_\_\_\_\_\_!

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:

* **their** is a possessive pronoun that means something *belongs to them*

 *The hockey players brought **their** pads and skates to the rink.*

* **there** is an adverb that describes a *specific location* or the *existence* of something

 *The dog park is over **there** by the lake.*

* **they're** is a contraction of the words *"they are"*

 *Asparagus stalks are usually green, but sometimes **they're** white.*
The sentence is identifying a *location*, so we should use **there**.

 *The hockey players brought **their** pads and skates to the rink.*

* **there** is an adverb that describes a *specific location* or the *existence* of something

 *The dog park is over **there** by the lake.*

* **they're** is a contraction of the words *"they are"*

 *Asparagus stalks are usually green, but sometimes **they're** white.*
The sentence needs to show *possession*, so we should use **their**.

khan_exercises.title: Frequently confused words: to/two/too
khan_exercises.description: Can you tell the difference between the words "to," "two," and "too"?

items.question_content:
**Identify whether the word _to_, _two_, or _too_ is the appropriate word in this sentence.**

>  The soup was \_\_\_\_\_\_ hot for eating yet.



[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
People often confuse the words "to", "too", and "two":

* **to** is a preposition that shows *direction or transition*

 *I drive **to** work each morning.*

* **too** is an adverb that can mean "also" or an *excess of something*

 *She drank **too **much orange juice and didn't feel well.*

* **two** is the written version of the number 2

 *Bob Dylan and Paul Simon are **two** of the most successful songwriters of all time.*
This sentence needs to show *excess*, so we should use **too**.

khan_exercises.title: Frequently confused words: assorted
khan_exercises.description: Can you identify when to correctly use these words that often get confused with other words?

items.question_content:
**Identify whether to use _accept_ or _except_ in this sentence.**

> Mrs. Gupta could not be here tonight, so I \_\_\_\_\_\_ this award on her behalf.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
People often confuse the words "accept" and "except":

* **accept** means "to receive", "to take in", or "to believe"

 *The scientist **accepted** the Nobel prize.*

* **except** means "not including" or "everything but"

 *I love vegetables, **except** for broccoli.*
This sentence needs a verb that means "to receive", so we should use **accept**.

items.question_content:
**Identify whether to use _accept_ or _except_ in this sentence.**

> Everyone was ready to leave for Space World amusement park \_\_\_\_\_\_ Eric, who was just waking up.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
People often confuse the words "accept" and "except":

* **accept** means "to receive", "to take in", or "to believe"

 *The scientist **accepted** the Nobel prize.*

* **except** means "not including" or "everything but"

 *I love vegetables, **except** for broccoli.*
This sentence needs a word that means "not including", so we should use **except**.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
People often confuse the words "principal" and "principle":

* **principal** can mean the person in charge of a school, or it can refer to anything that's *the most important*
* **principle** refers to a rule, law, or guideline
This sentence refers to the head of a school, so we should use **principal**.
